title: The Battle of Plassey
summary: The East India Company becomes a territorial power.
what: Company forces defeated the Nawab of Bengal and gained decisive influence over a wealthy province.
why: Commercial power turned into colonial rule, making private corporate violence central to the origins of the Raj.
colle: Can a private company become an empire?

title: The Rebellion of 1857
summary: A vast uprising ends Company rule.
what: Indian soldiers and civilians rebelled across northern and central India in a conflict remembered variously as a mutiny or a war of independence.
why: Britain transferred power to the Crown and reorganised the Raj.
colle: How does the name given to a rebellion shape historical memory?

title: The Indian National Congress is founded
summary: An all-India political organisation emerges.
what: Educated Indian leaders created a forum that initially sought reform within the Empire.
why: Congress later became the principal movement for mass anti-colonial nationalism.
colle: How do moderate reform movements become revolutionary?

title: Partition of Bengal and Swadeshi
summary: British administrative division provokes mass resistance.
what: The partition of Bengal was opposed through boycotts, indigenous production and political mobilisation.
why: The Swadeshi movement broadened nationalism but also sharpened communal and regional tensions.
colle: Can economic nationalism unite a diverse society?

title: The Amritsar massacre
summary: British troops kill unarmed civilians at Jallianwala Bagh.
what: General Dyer ordered fire on a trapped crowd, causing hundreds of deaths.
why: The massacre destroyed faith in gradual imperial reform for many Indians.
colle: Can an empire retain legitimacy after state violence?

title: The Salt March
summary: Gandhi turns salt into a symbol of civil disobedience.
what: Marchers defied the colonial salt monopoly through a carefully staged non-violent campaign.
why: The action demonstrated the political power of mass participation, symbolism and media attention.
colle: Is non-violent resistance more effective than armed struggle?

title: The Quit India Movement
summary: Congress demands immediate British withdrawal.
what: Leaders were arrested as protests and repression spread during the Second World War.
why: Although suppressed, the movement showed that post-war imperial rule would be unsustainable.
colle: Did the Second World War make Indian independence inevitable?

title: Independence and Partition
summary: Freedom arrives with mass displacement and killing.
what: British India was divided into India and Pakistan, prompting one of history’s largest forced migrations.
why: Partition remains central to relations with Pakistan, minority politics and the Kashmir dispute.
colle: Was Partition the unavoidable price of independence?

title: The Constitution
summary: India becomes a sovereign democratic republic.
what: The constitution established universal adult suffrage, federalism, fundamental rights and a secular state.
why: It created democratic institutions on an extraordinary scale in a poor, diverse and recently partitioned country.
colle: How has India sustained democracy despite immense diversity?

title: The first general election
summary: Hundreds of millions participate in universal suffrage.
what: India organised a vast multi-phase election despite poverty, illiteracy and limited infrastructure.
why: The success gave democratic legitimacy to the republic and normalised electoral competition.
colle: Why did democracy take root in India?

title: War and the creation of Bangladesh
summary: India defeats Pakistan in a decisive regional war.
what: India intervened after mass displacement and conflict in East Pakistan, which became Bangladesh.
why: The victory confirmed India’s regional power but deepened rivalry with Pakistan.
colle: Can humanitarian intervention be separated from strategic interest?

title: The Emergency
summary: Democratic freedoms are suspended.
what: Indira Gandhi’s government ruled by decree, censored the press and detained opponents.
why: Voters later removed her from office, making the period both a warning about authoritarianism and evidence of democratic resilience.
colle: Could India’s democracy survive another Emergency?

title: Operation Blue Star and Indira Gandhi’s assassination
summary: Conflict in Punjab produces national trauma.
what: The army entered the Golden Temple to remove militants; Sikh bodyguards later assassinated the Prime Minister, followed by anti-Sikh massacres.
why: The year exposed the dangers of religious conflict, state force and impunity.
colle: How should democracies respond to separatist violence without targeting communities?

title: Economic liberalisation
summary: India turns away from a heavily regulated economy.
what: A balance-of-payments crisis prompted deregulation, lower trade barriers and greater foreign investment.
why: Reform accelerated growth and created a large middle class while widening regional and social inequalities.
colle: Has liberalisation benefited India fairly?

title: The demolition of the Babri Masjid
summary: Hindu nationalist mobilisation reaches a turning point.
what: A Hindu nationalist crowd destroyed the mosque at Ayodhya, triggering communal violence.
why: The event accelerated the rise of the BJP and made religion even more central to national politics.
colle: Can India remain both Hindu-majority and secular?

title: Nuclear tests
summary: India openly declares itself a nuclear-armed power.
what: The government conducted tests at Pokhran, followed by Pakistani tests.
why: Nuclear weapons enhanced status and deterrence while increasing regional risk.
colle: Do nuclear weapons make South Asia safer?

title: The Right to Information Act
summary: Citizens gain a powerful anti-corruption tool.
what: The law allowed people to request records from public authorities.
why: It strengthened transparency and grassroots accountability, although later implementation and institutional independence became contested.
colle: Can transparency laws overcome entrenched corruption?

title: The Mumbai attacks
summary: Coordinated terrorism strikes India’s financial capital.
what: Ten attackers targeted hotels, a railway station and other sites, killing more than 160 people.
why: The attacks reshaped security policy and intensified India-Pakistan tensions.
colle: How should democracies respond to cross-border terrorism?

title: Narendra Modi comes to power
summary: The BJP wins a parliamentary majority.
what: Modi campaigned on development, strong leadership and Hindu nationalist themes.
why: The election began a dominant political era that reshaped federalism, media, welfare and the meaning of secularism.
colle: Has Modi strengthened India or weakened its pluralism?

title: Demonetisation
summary: Most high-value banknotes are suddenly withdrawn.
what: The government presented the measure as an attack on undeclared wealth and counterfeit currency.
why: The disruption accelerated digital payments but its economic benefits remain heavily disputed.
colle: Was demonetisation bold reform or avoidable disruption?

title: The Goods and Services Tax
summary: A national indirect tax seeks to create one market.
what: GST replaced many state and federal taxes with a unified system.
why: The reform strengthened economic integration but created complexity for small businesses and federal tensions.
colle: Can economic centralisation coexist with Indian federalism?

title: Article 370 is abrogated
summary: Jammu and Kashmir loses its special constitutional status.
what: The government divided the former state into two union territories and imposed sweeping security restrictions.
why: Supporters called it integration; critics saw an attack on federalism, rights and Kashmiri consent.
colle: Can national integration be imposed without local consent?

title: Farm laws and mass protest
summary: Farmers force the repeal of major market reforms.
what: Three agricultural laws prompted a year-long protest movement around Delhi.
why: Their withdrawal showed that mass mobilisation can still constrain a strong majority government.
colle: How powerful is civil society under a dominant government?

title: Population, G20 and the Moon
summary: India’s global profile rises sharply.
what: India became the world’s most populous country, hosted the G20 summit and landed Chandrayaan-3 near the Moon’s south pole.
why: The achievements reinforced claims that India is an emerging technological and diplomatic power.
colle: Can India convert global prestige into broad domestic development?

title: Modi wins a third term without a BJP majority
summary: Coalition politics returns at the national level.
what: The BJP remained the largest party but lost its single-party majority; the NDA coalition kept Narendra Modi in office.
why: The result checked BJP dominance while confirming Modi’s continuing centrality.
colle: Did the 2024 election strengthen Indian democracy?
